names.japanese_name: ヘキサメチレンテトラミン

natural_occurrence.description: BANNED in EU and USA but still used in some countries. Breaks down into formaldehyde in acidic conditions - major safety concern. Also used as fuel tablets for camping stoves (same chemical!). Historical use dates back to early 1900s. One of the most controversial preservatives still in use anywhere. The formaldehyde formed is the reason for the ban in most countries. Example of a preservative being phased out globally.

manufacturing.description: Synthesized by reacting formaldehyde with ammonia under controlled conditions.

other_uses.industrial: Used in production of resins, rubber additives, and explosives
other_uses.pharmaceutical: Historical use as urinary antiseptic (rarely used now)
other_uses.household: Fuel tablets for camping stoves (hexamine stove fuel)

eu.conditions: Removed from approved list for food use due to formaldehyde formation

usa.classification: Not approved by FDA for food use
usa.notes: Not approved due to formaldehyde decomposition concerns

japan.japanese_regulation_name: ヘキサメチレンテトラミン
japan.notes: Approved with restrictions for specific foods only (特定の食品のみ)

australia_nz.notes: Approved for provolone cheese only (traditional use grandfathered in)

canada.notes: Restricted use; limited to specific traditional applications

regulatory_history.event: Hexamine first used as food preservative in traditional cheese production
Research identifies formaldehyde formation from hexamine in acidic conditions
EU begins phasing out hexamine from food approvals due to carcinogen concerns
EU removes hexamine from approved food additives list
Remains banned in most developed countries; some traditional uses grandfathered in limited regions

food_categories_eu.category_name: BANNED - not approved for any food category

commonly_found_in: Caviar (in some countries where still legal)
BANNED in most food applications globally

adi_context.source_note: CRITICAL WARNING: Decomposes into formaldehyde (a known carcinogen) in acidic conditions. No established ADI due to safety concerns. Banned in EU and USA. Still used in some traditional cheese production where grandfathered in. One of the most controversial preservatives.
